rationale_for_baseline_tier: A 45-minute uncontrolled high-volume epistaxis (>150 mL estimated, three soaked towels) unresponsive to first aid, with new pallor, lethargy and postural dizziness (early hypovolaemic shock), in a ~19 kg child who has a documented inherited coagulation-factor deficiency and cannot form a stable clot, and with blood tracking into the pharynx. This is a time-critical haemorrhagic emergency with airway risk; the child cannot self-arrest the bleed and definitive haemostasis plus possible factor replacement require immediate in-person emergency care. T5 (call 000 / emergency ambulance) is the correct tier, with carer-deliverable first aid in the interim.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Heavy nosebleed still not controlled after 10–15 minutes of correct firm pressure in a child with a known clotting-factor deficiency
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — do not wait; call 000 while continuing pressure
escalation_edges.rationale: Uncontrolled haemorrhage in a coagulopathic child will not reliably self-limit and can progress to decompensated shock.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Call 000 for an ambulance right now. Keep him sitting up and leaning forward, and keep pinching the soft part of his nose firmly without letting go. Tell them he has a bleeding disorder and the nosebleed won't stop.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Increasing pallor, floppiness/drowsiness, dizziness or fainting — signs of significant blood loss
escalation_edges.rationale: Systemic signs indicate early hypovolaemic shock; a child compensates then decompensates abruptly, so emergency ambulance care is required now.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because he's gone pale, floppy and dizzy, this is an emergency — call 000 now. Lay him on his side if he becomes very drowsy so any blood drains out of his mouth, and stay with him.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting a large amount of dark (swallowed) blood, or becoming difficult to wake / confused
escalation_edges.rationale: Haematemesis of swallowed blood plus falling conscious level signals large occult loss and airway risk — emergency resuscitation needed.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he vomits a lot of dark blood, or becomes hard to wake or confused, this is a 000 emergency — call now and put him on his side to protect his airway.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: After hospital stabilisation: any restart of bleeding not stopping within 15 minutes of correct pressure at home
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Within the 48 hours after the event
escalation_edges.rationale: The clot is fragile in a coagulopathic child; a re-bleed needs re-presentation rather than home management.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the bleeding starts again later and doesn't stop after 15 minutes of pinching the soft part of his nose, go straight back to the emergency department.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Inherited Factor XI deficiency plus pulmonary valve stenosis
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lowers the threshold for escalation: the child cannot form a stable clot and his fixed cardiac outflow obstruction limits compensation for blood loss — treat any significant bleed as an emergency and involve haematology early.

Children maintain blood pressure until late then decompensate rapidly; rely on tachycardia, pallor, capillary refill and behaviour, not BP, and escalate early. No weight-based dose is to be synthesised — flag for in-person/specialist dosing.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance now — tell them a child with a bleeding disorder has a nosebleed that won't stop and he's gone pale and dizzy. While you wait: sit him upright and lean him forward, pinch the soft part of his nose firmly for 10–15 minutes without letting go, and have him spit out blood rather than swallow it. Keep him calm and still, and unlock the door for the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours or the planned telehealth review.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Hospital will document the bleed, blood loss, any tranexamic acid/factor treatment given and coagulation results — take these to the GP and paediatric haematologist. Reinstate a home supply of topical tranexamic acid and reinforce correct first-aid technique.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating an uncontrolled bleed with early-shock signs in a coagulopathic child as a routine nosebleed manageable at home or via a GP appointment.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Ongoing haemorrhage progresses to decompensated hypovolaemic shock and cardiovascular collapse; delayed factor correction and possible airway compromise from swallowed blood.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Directing self-transport to ED rather than an ambulance, when active shock signs and airway risk warrant paramedic care en route (deterioration during private transport without monitoring, resuscitation or airway support).

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Over-triage is not a meaningful risk here — the presentation genuinely warrants the highest tier.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Exact vital signs, haemoglobin and true (including swallowed) blood loss cannot be measured via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the combination of prolonged uncontrolled bleeding, systemic signs and a known coagulopathy as an emergency and escalate; do not wait for a measurement that only in-person care can provide.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Using a reassuring-looking blood pressure or the familiarity of 'a nosebleed' to downgrade urgency and defer to watchful waiting.
